perturbation.original_text: In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that for a period of five (5) years commencing on the date of this Agreement, CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR that is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: In view of TSR's desire that such documentation and information be maintained in confidence, it is hereby agreed that for a period of one (1) year commencing on the date of this Agreement, CGS shall not disclose or otherwise make available to a third party any proprietary information and documentation received from TSR that is identified as confidential ("Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year might be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable state laws, creating a potential legal contradiction. State laws generally afford greater protection to trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (varies by state, e.g., California)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act, adopted in many states including California, protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year could be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information retains its value beyond that period. One year might be challenged as unreasonably short. This contradicts the open-ended protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA as long as secrecy is maintained.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act :: Civil Code :: 2010 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2010 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 California Code Civil Code Title 5. Uniform Trade Secrets Act CIVIL CODE SECTION 3426-3426.11 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, limited liability company, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (d) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (1) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (2) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances t
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation states that limiting the confidentiality period to one year could be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information retains its value beyond that period, potentially contradicting the open-ended protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA as long as secrecy is maintained. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'trade secret' and discusses 'misappropriation,' emphasizing the importance of maintaining secrecy. Therefore, reducing the confidentiality period creates a potential legal contradiction as it might not provide sufficient protection for trade secrets under the UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be promptly returned to TSR upon request or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be returned to TSR within 90 days of request or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly returned' to 'returned to TSR within 90 days of request' may violate trade secret protection laws that require immediate action to protect confidential information. A delay of 90 days could be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. Delaying the return of confidential information by 90 days after a request could be seen as providing an opportunity for misappropriation, thereby undermining the immediate protective measures expected under the law.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 'prompt' return of confidential information. The changed text allows for a 90-day delay. This delay introduces an inconsistency that creates a potential violation of trade secret laws. As per the Law Explanation, the Economic Espionage Act aims to protect trade secrets, and a delay could provide opportunity for misappropriation. The modified text directly contradicts the need for immediate protective measures, potentially violating 18 USC 1832 which addresses the theft of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: CGS agrees not to pursue independent business relations with any of TSR's clients for which it provides services for a minimum period of two (2) years from the end date of the program-specific Agreement(s)
perturbation.changed_text: CGS agrees not to pursue independent business relations with any of TSR's clients for which it provides services for a minimum period of ten (10) years from the end date of the program-specific Agreement(s)
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to ten years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under many state laws, which typically limit non-compete agreements to shorter durations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While there are narrow exceptions, a ten-year non-compete is likely to be considered an unlawful restraint of trade in California. Most other states have similar, if less strict, limitations on the duration of non-compete clauses, rendering a ten-year term suspect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from two to ten years. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both state that such a long non-compete period is likely unenforceable and an unlawful restraint of trade, particularly in states like California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Business and Professions Code Section 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," with some exceptions not elaborated on here. The changed text, by extending the non-compete to ten years, creates a more substantial restraint than the original two years, thus strengthening the likelihood of violating the law and creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be promptly returned to TSR upon request or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information transmitted hereunder shall be and remain the property of TSR, and all such Information and any copies thereof shall be returned to TSR upon request when CGS deems it commercially reasonable or destroyed at TSR's option.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'when CGS deems it commercially reasonable' provides CGS with excessive discretion regarding the return of confidential information, potentially conflicting with obligations to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets and allows companies to sue for misappropriation. Inserting a 'commercially reasonable' standard that gives CGS broad discretion in returning confidential information could weaken TSR's ability to enforce its rights under the DTSA, as it creates ambiguity and potential delays in recovering its trade secrets, hindering effective protection.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 'commercially reasonable' standard that allows CGS discretion in returning confidential information to TSR. This contradicts the spirit of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) primarily outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, the addition of a commercially reasonable standard weakens TSR's ability to promptly recover its trade secrets, potentially hindering its rights under the DTSA. The original text mandates prompt return, while the modified text allows for delay based on CGS's assessment of commercial reasonability. This condition undermines the protection that the DTSA is intended to provide, creating a legal contradiction.
